title: Grapheme Choice and Orthographic Principles
subtitle: Why English Spells the Way It Does
summary: Explores how grapheme choice in English is principled, not arbitrary. Teachers learn to analyze why specific graphemes appear in specific positions and how morphemic structure, etymology, and phonological context govern spelling. Dismantles the myth that English spelling is chaotic by revealing the orthographic principles at work.
learning_outcomes: Explain why English grapheme choice is principled rather than arbitrary
Analyze grapheme function in context using the Four Questions
Identify how morphemic boundaries influence grapheme choice
Recognize the role of etymology in explaining grapheme patterns
Replace deficit framings (silent letters, exceptions) with structural explanations
key_concepts: No silent letters — describe function instead
swi_connections: Deepens Question 4: How do the graphemes function?
Connects grapheme analysis to morphemic structure from Q2 and Q3
Demonstrates how grapheme choice serves meaning, not just sound
int_connections: Principled explanations reduce working memory load vs. rote memorization
Structural reasoning supports learners who struggle with arbitrary rules
Predictable grapheme logic increases learner confidence and regulation

sections.title: The chaos that isn't
sections.summary: The received claim that English spelling is irregular or largely arbitrary. What a principled examination shows: most 'exceptions' are words whose explanatory framework hasn't been found yet. The module's central commitment: every spelling has a reason — position, morphology, or etymology. The task is always to find it.

sections.title: Position as a constraint on grapheme choice
sections.summary: Certain graphemes appear only in specific positions. <ck> follows a short vowel at the end of a single-syllable base; <tch> follows a short vowel where <ch> would appear elsewhere. Position — not arbitrariness — explains why one grapheme appears and not another. Worked examples across several grapheme pairs.

sections.title: Morphemic boundaries and grapheme selection
sections.summary: When a word belongs to a morphological family, the base constrains spelling across the entire family. <sign>, <signal>, <signature>, <significant>: the <g> is stable because it is part of the base, not because of where it falls phonologically. Morphemic membership, not phonemic context, is the primary determinant of grapheme choice.

sections.title: Etymology as the third explanatory layer
sections.summary: When position and morphology don't fully explain a spelling, etymology provides the third framework. French-origin: <ch> → /ʃ/ in <machine>, <chef>, <champagne>. Greek-origin: <ph> → /f/ in <phone>; <ch> → /k/ in <chorus>, <chaos>. Etymology is not trivia — it is a principled explanatory system with consistent patterns.

sections.title: Grapheme function versus grapheme name
sections.summary: Asking 'what sound does <ea> make?' presupposes a single answer. Asking 'what is the function of <ea> in this word?' opens analysis: /ɛ/ in <bread>, /iː/ in <bead>, /eɪ/ in <great>. Function is always contextual. The shift from naming to analyzing function is the core analytical move of Q4.

sections.title: Spelling stability across pronunciation shifts
sections.summary: Morphemes hold their spelling across phonological environments. <electric/electricity>: the <c> is stable across /k/ and /s/. <sane/sanity>, <divine/divinity>: vowel shifts do not disturb the base spelling. This principle — established in Module 1 for <-ed> — extends across the entire writing system. Stability is the rule.

sections.title: From deficit framings to structural explanations
sections.summary: A practicum in replacing common deficit framings with structural analysis. The <b> in <debt> (Latin debitum; family: dubitable). The <k> in <know> (Germanic, still present in <knowledge>). The <gh> in <light> (historical fricative still audible in <laughter>). Each so-called silent letter has a function; the question is always what it is.
